coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal preparation of highly stable N doped oxidized carbon dots for toxic organic pollutants sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Bogireddy, Naveen Kumar Reddy
Lara, Joel
Fragoso, Lourdes Rodriguez
Agarwal, Vivechana
coredata.dc:description: A simple one-pot hydrothermally fabricated N- doped oxidized carbon dots (NOCDs) revealed an excellent photostability under constant illumination with 344 nm UV radiation for 24,000 s and intermittently tested stability under ambient conditions for over 18 months. The general susceptibility of their PL was tested under different conditions of pH and temperature. The NOCDs were found to act as optical fluorometric sensors for pH and temperature revealing a linear, cyclic and reversible trend in their fluorescence intensity between pH 3 and pH 9 (response time ≤60 s) and 25 °C–70 °C temperature range (response time: ≤240 s). As reasonable temperature and pH sensing agent, a good linear fit with an excellent regression coefficients of 0.979 and 0.989 respectively, were revealed. Typical organic pollutants such as anionic (methyl Orange (MO) and Bromophenol blue (BrPB)) and cationic (Methylene Blue (MB), Rhodamine 6 G (Rh6G)) dyes were found to quench the corresponding fluorescence through an inner filter effect. This is the first report on detection of organic pollutants (MO, Rh6G and BrPB) using NOCDs as optical sensing probe. In vitro imaging on three different kinds of cells (HEK293, A549 and MDA-MB-231) confirms their biocombatibility. The work represents great opportunities from experimental research towards the practical applications with a possibility to develop them as highly stable fluorescent imaging agents for the applications in bio-imaging and sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Toxic pollutants
Optical sensing
Bioimaging
Stability

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide dots (NOCDs)
Optical pH sensing and zeta potential measurements
Fluorescence quenching effect (sensitivity) of organic dyes on NOCDs signal intensity
Morphological analysis and identification of NOCDs inside cells
Sensing of organic pollutants using NOCDs probe
Effect of pH on organic dyes sensing
Effect of NOCDs’ concentration on sensing of organic dyes
Possible mechanism for the sensing of organic pollutants
Biocompatibility studies of NOCDs in tumor and non-tumor cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: VA acknowledges the financial grant from CONACyT Basic Sciences project A1-S-30393.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HR-TEM images of as-prepared NOCDs, (b) XRD patterns of NOCDs, (c) XPS survey scan and de-convoluted XPS spectra of (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s and (f) O1s peaks of NOCDs.

Optical properties of as-prepared NOCDs (a) Fluorescence studies under different excitation wavelengths from 250 to 290 nm and (b) 295 to 420 nm, (c) absorbance (blue) and PLE (black, λem = 441 nm) studies (5 µL of NOCDs in 400 µL of DI water), and (d) Fluorescence studies under different concentration of NOCDs (0–14 µL) in DI water, total volume is maintained with 405 µL. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Stability test of NOCDs (a) PL studies under continuous excitation for 24,000 s, (b) percentage change in PL signal intensity at different time periods; up to 18 months span (inset shows the digital photographs under UV lamp with 365 nm excitation), (c) UV–visible absorbance and PLE studies of NOCDs at different time intervals and (d) digital photographs of NOCDs in day light at 3 time intervals (6, 12 and 18 months).

NOCDs based optical temperature sensor: (a) florescence spectra and their corresponding (b) temperature vs. PL intensity of NOCDs at different temperatures ranging from 25 to 70 °C with the step of 5 °C, and (c) multiple cycle reversibility test by changing NOCDs temperature from 25 to 70 °C.

NOCDs based optical pH sensor: (a) Fluorescence spectra of NOCDs at different pH values from 2 to 9, (b) shows a linear relationship (pH 3 to 9) with NOCDs pH vs PL intensity at 344 nm excitation, (c) reversibility test between pH 3 and 9 under normalized PL measurements, and (d) Zeta potential measurements of NOCDs at different pH values.

Selectivity of organic dyes (Methylene blue, methyl orange, rhodamine 6 g and bromophenol blue) using NOCDs as probes using PL spectroscopy, corresponding normalized graph at λem = 441 nm (F0 = NOCDs fluorescence signal intensity in deionized water and F1 = fluorescence signal intensity of same concentration of NOCDs in the presence of analytes). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence studies of cationic dyes (a) methylene blue (MB) and (b) rhodamine 6G, and anionic dyes (c) methyl orange (MO) and (d) bromophenol blue (BrPB) respectively (λex = 344 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Analysis (organic dye concentration vs PL intensity vs emission wavelength(λem)) from fluorescence spectra of NOCDs with the addition of different concentration of organic dyes (a) Methyl Orange (MO), (b) Methylene blue (MB), (c) Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) and (d) Bromophenol blue (BrPB). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Variation of organic dye concentration vs PL peak emission wavelength/FWHM) from fluorescence spectra of NOCDs with the addition of different concentration of organic dyes (a) Methyl Orange (MO), (b) Methylene blue (MB), (c) Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) and (d) Bromophenol blue (BrPB). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Absorbance spectra of NOCDs with the addition of different concentration of organic dyes (a) Methyl Orange (MO), (b) Methylene blue (MB), (c) Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) and (d) Bromophenol blue (BrPB). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots (NOCDs) as efficient sensing probe for the detection of organic pollutants at different concentrations of (a) methylene blue, (b) methyl orange, (c) rhodamine 6G and (d) bromophenol blue respectively, as a function of pH range from 3 to 9. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots as efficient sensing probe for the detection of organic pollutants as a function of (a) pH values 3, 5, 7, and 9, vs slope values obtained from the Fig. 11 and (b) their corresponding minimum detection limits of methylene blue, methyl orange, rhodamine 6G and bromophenol blue respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots (NOCDs) as efficient sensing probe for the detection of organic pollutants at different concentrations of (a) methylene blue, (b) methyl orange, (c) rhodamine 6G and (d) bromophenol blue respectively, as a function of NOCDs concentration. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots as efficient sensing probe for the detection of organic pollutants as a function of (a) NOCDs concentration vs slope values obtained from the Fig. 13 and (b) their corresponding minimum detection limits of methylene blue, methyl orange, rhodamine 6G and bromophenol blue respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of NOCDs on cell viability of human cell lines. Cells were exposed to different concentrations of NOCDs for 24 h. Bars express percentage of cell viability as compared with control group. Results are presented as mean ± SD of at least two experiments.

Microscopic (optical; upper part) and fluorescence emission images (fluorescence microscope; 40x magnification; lower part) for studying the effect of NOCDs on HEK-293, A549 and MDA-MB-231 cells’ morphology. The cells were treated at different concentrations of 0.1, 1, 10, 100, 500, 1000 µg/mL of probe solution for 24 h and stained with Giemsa staining (Total number of cells per well: 50,000 (optical images)and 150,000 (fluorescence images)),scale bar = 20 µm.

Schematic representation of the photo stability over 18 months, as temperature and pH sensors and moreover, mechanistic aspects of optical detection of organic pollutants using nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots (NOCDs) as sustainable sensing nanoprobe.
Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values as function of organic dyes pH values in the presence of nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots.
Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values as function of nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots concentration in organic dyes sensing.
Cell proliferation rate after treatment as a function of NOCDs concentration (0.1 to 1000 µg/mL) and time (24 to 96 h).

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal preparation of highly stable N doped oxidized carbon dots for toxic organic pollutants sensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas, UAEM, Av. Univ. 1001, Col. Chamilpa, Cuernavaca, Morelos 62209, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas, UAEM, Av. Univ. 1001, Col. Chamilpa, Cuernavaca, Morelos 62209, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Facultad de Farmacia, Universidad Autónoma del Estado de Morelos, Cuernavaca 62210, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidad Autónoma del Estado de Morelos

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Facultad de Farmacia, Universidad Autónoma del Estado de Morelos, Cuernavaca 62210, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple one-pot hydrothermally fabricated N- doped oxidized carbon dots (NOCDs) revealed an excellent photostability under constant illumination with 344 nm UV radiation for 24,000 s and intermittently tested stability under ambient conditions for over 18 months. The general susceptibility of their PL was tested under different conditions of pH and temperature. The NOCDs were found to act as optical fluorometric sensors for pH and temperature revealing a linear, cyclic and reversible trend in their fluorescence intensity between pH 3 and pH 9 (response time ≤60 s) and 25 °C–70 °C temperature range (response time: ≤240 s). As reasonable temperature and pH sensing agent, a good linear fit with an excellent regression coefficients of 0.979 and 0.989 respectively, were revealed. Typical organic pollutants such as anionic (methyl Orange (MO) and Bromophenol blue (BrPB)) and cationic (Methylene Blue (MB), Rhodamine 6 G (Rh6G)) dyes were found to quench the corresponding fluorescence through an inner filter effect. This is the first report on detection of organic pollutants (MO, Rh6G and BrPB) using NOCDs as optical sensing probe. In vitro imaging on three different kinds of cells (HEK293, A549 and MDA-MB-231) confirms their biocombatibility. The work represents great opportunities from experimental research towards the practical applications with a possibility to develop them as highly stable fluorescent imaging agents for the applications in bio-imaging and sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon based materials, namely graphene, fullerene, and carbon nano tubes, due to their high performance in optoelectronic applications, have attracted attention around the world. The first accidental discovery of luminescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) was during the separation and purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1]. Fluorescent CNPs/carbon dots (CDots) are relatively new members of carbon-based nanomaterials with the size ≤10 nm and their functional group rich surface [2]. As compared with the conventional binary compounds based semiconductor quantum dots (i.e., cadmium sulfide, cadmium selenide, indium arsenide, indium phosphide, lead selenide and lead sulfide) and organic fluorescent dyes (Fluorophore, rhodamine B and Rhodamine 6G etc.,), CDots have attractive fluorescence properties, including photostability, biocompatibility and no bleaching, and moreover, excitation-dependent and independent fluorescence emission [3,4]. In addition, due to outstanding properties, such as, low cytotoxicity, easy functionalization, good chemical stability, catalytic properties and good water solubility, fluorescent CDots have been widely applied in various applications, such as drug delivery [5,6], optoelectronic devices [7–9], biosensing [10,11], bioimaging [12–14] and catalysis [15,16]. Owing to these impressive properties and extensive applications, numerous effective methods of synthesizing fluorescent CDots have been developed, which can be divided into top-down and bottom-up [17,18]. A series of top-down methods used to obtain CDots from relatively large carbon structures consist of physical or chemical cutting treatment, such as laser ablation [19], chemical oxidation [20,21], electro chemical oxidation [22] and arc discharge [1]. On the other hand, bottom-up methods to fabricate fluorescent CDots have also gained increasing attention. Briefly, microwave irradiation, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal treatment, and plasma treatment were used to fabricate fluorescent CDots by selecting conventional organic molecules as carbon precursor [23]. The fluorescent CDots have been effectively fabricated using wide variety of organic molecules, such as, dopamine [24], glucose [25,26], chitosan [27], amino acids [28], and citric acid [29] etc.

Interestingly, irrespective of the organic molecule as precursors used, most of the CDots are found to demonstrate extensive similarities in their fluorescence properties. In addition to their excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence spectra [30,31], the fluorescence of CDots is also sensitive to external parameters, like, temperature, pH and the nature of the solvent medium [32,33]. With regard to temperature and pH, it has been observed that in some cases the highest fluorescent signal intensity of CDots occur in neutral conditions, whereas in others, a gradual increase in fluorescence signal intensity has been obtained on varying from acidic to basic or increasing the temperature from RT to high temperatures or vice versa [20,34,35]. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission wavelength changes with pH and temperatures of CDots, while in others it remains unchanged [36].

Highly toxic emerging organic pollutants and heavy metals are a major threat to the environment since many agricultural and textile industries release huge amounts of highly polluted water that naturally consist of synthetic organic dyes and pollutants [37–42]. The existence of such contaminants makes the water purification difficult due to their high chemical stability in aqueous medium [41,42]. Hence, several efforts made to detect and quantify the amount of organic dyes such as methylene blue, methyl orange, rhodamine 6G and bromophenol blue due to their typical dangerous toxicity levels 1.2, 60.0, 4.0 and >2000.0 mg/kg respectively [43,44]. Till now, there are several techniques to detect organic dyes such as capillary electrophoresis [43], spectrophotometry [45], and liquid chromatography [46]. Some of these methods have the practical disadvantage of being expensive, low sensitivity, time consuming and are unsuitable for microanalysis, whereas the spectrophotometric technique is portable, rapid, and simple, so it is one of the best suitable method for detecting organic dyes in industrial level.

In addition, the sensing of toxic organic pollutants such as methylene blue, methyl orange, rhodamine 6G and bromophenol blue in contaminated water remains a challenge. Most of the recently reported fluorescence probes [47,48] reveal low photostability and biocompatibility. In order to overcome these difficulties, highly stable and biocompatible sensing probes are required to detect contaminants in industrial water. Use of sustainable precursors [49] such as citric acid, urea and parts of plants to fabricate carbon dots (as sensing probes), where suspended contaminants can be detected in minimal concentrations, makes the process economical and sustainable.

Furthermore, unlike semiconductor-based quantum dots or organic dyes, the CDots owe metal-free carbon precursor during synthesis and exhibit photostability along with high in vitro as well as in vivo biocompatibility [50]. Cellular or sub-cellular labelling of the cells is of great interest in biology where specifically nucleus has been regarded as one of the main targets for cancer therapeutics [51]. Selective nucleus labelling can help to develop advanced and selective active-targeting drug delivery systems [51,52]. However, most of the systems available as of date (discussed earlier) suffer from serious limitations such as photobleaching in organic dyes or cytotoxicity related to semiconductor quantum dots. The CDots-based systems serve as a good alternative to nucleus staining due to good cellular distribution, non-photobleaching and multi-photon emission [53–55].

This report describes the interesting pH and temperature dependent fluorescence properties of as-prepared highly stable NOCDs. The pH and temperature induced changes in the optical properties of these NOCDs not only reveal the stability but also multiple cycle reversibility by changing pH from 3 to 9 and temperature from 25 to 70 °C. NOCDs were used to detect several toxic organic pollutants like methylene blue, methyl orange, rhodamine 6G and bromophenol blue. Furthermore, NOCDs were tested for cell viability and proliferation assays for three different kinds of cells, i.e., HEK293, A549 and MDA-MB-231 cells.

Citric acid (C6H8O7), urea ((NH2)2CO), methylene blue (MB), Methylene orange (MO), Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G), Bromophenol blue (BrPB), glucose, sucrose, fructose, ZnCl2, NiCl2, FeCl2, MgCl2, AgNO3, CoCl2, CuCl2, MnCl2 ions, arsenic, L-Ascorbic acid, L-Cystine, L-glutamic acid, 2-aminophenol, 4-aminophenol, catechol, phenol, terbium and gadolinium were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Phosphate buffers were bought from Golden Bell, Mexico and deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) used throughout the experiments.

HEK-293 cells (ATCC® CRL-1573), A549 cells (ATCC® CRM-CCL-185) and MDA-MB-231 cells (ATCC® HTB-26) were cultured in MEM (GIBCO®, USA), HAM F12 (Sigma Aldrich, USA) and DMEM HG (GIBCO®, USA) respectively. All media were complemented with 10% FBS (GIBCO®, USA), in humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide dots (NOCDs)
ce:section.ce:para:
NOCDs were fabricated using already reported hydrothermal method [56]. Briefly, 0.2 g citric acid and 0.38 g urea used as carbon and nitrogen precursors are dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. The mixer solution was kept under constant stirring for 20 min to make clear solution and kept in Teflon autoclave at 180 °C for 1 h. NOCDs were centrifuged and supernent was stored in dark at RT for further usage and the optical characterization was showed in the Figs. 1 and 2
.

UV–visible absorbance spectra and fluorescence spectra of as-prepared NOCDs were monitored with dual beam Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 and Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, respectively. The particle size distribution was calculated using transmission electron microscopy measurements (JOEL JEM-ARM200F). Diffraction pattern of NOCDs was measured using Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600 photoelectron spectrometer was used to carry out surface sensitive quantitative analysis, with Al Kα radiation photon energy (1486.6 ± 0.2 eV). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of NOCDs in aqueous medium were measured using Varian 660-IR (ATR) FT-IR spectrophotometer.

Photostability of fluorescent NOCDs was tested by a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a (pulsed) Xenon lamp at λex = 334 nm and λem = 441 nm with a scan time of 400 min. The excitation and emission slit width were kept at 5 nm and average time for each measurement was kept as 0.1 s under continuous monitoring. Furthermore, optical properties of highly stable NOCDs were measured over a time period of 18 months using UV–vis absorbance and Fluorescence Spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical pH sensing and zeta potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 400 µg/mL NOCDs were adjusted to different pH values using phosphate buffers for sensitivity and reversibility experiments. Photoluminescence measurements were performed at excitation wavelength of 344 nm, and its emission was analyzed at different pH values. The cyclic switching corresponding to pH values between pH 2 and pH 3 was done by continuous phosphate buffer titration. Further, zeta potential measurements were carried out at all pH values.

The temperature sensing measurements were carried out using similar NOCDs’ concentration as used for pH sensing. Briefly, 400 µg/mL NOCDs were adjusted to different temperature values using heating mantle for sensitivity and reversibility experiments. Photoluminescence measurements were performed at excitation wavelength of 344 nm, and its emission was analyzed at different temperatures. The reversibility switching in the temperature values was analyzed between 25 and 70 °C.

Similar to our previous reports [57], the relative selectivity of NOCDs was tested on some common analytical systems. The fluorescence signal intensity of NOCDs was studied under identical conditions/concentrations of 4 mg/mL NOCDs solution with 0.25 mM glucose, sucrose, fructose, ZnCl2, NiCl2, FeCl2, MgCl2, AgNO3, CoCl2, CuCl2, MnCl2 ions, arsenic, L-Ascorbic acid, L-Cystine, L-glutamic acid, 2-aminophenol, 4-aminophenol, catechol, phenol, terbium, gadolinium, methylene blue, methyl orange, rhodamine 6G, and Bromophenol blue. All of the analytes are of analytical grade and used as received from Sigma-Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching effect (sensitivity) of organic dyes on NOCDs signal intensity
ce:section.ce:para: A series of organic pollutant solutions with different concentrations (10-3 to 10-9 M) were added in 5 μL of as-prepared NOCDs solution, where the overall volume was maintained to be 405 μL and the solution was agitated well to form uniform solution. After 5 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 441 nm (excitation wavelength). The organic dyes sensing efficiency was additionally tested as a function of probe (NOCDs) concentration and pH values.

Cell viability and proliferation were carried out using methyl tetrazolium, (Sigma Aldrich, USA) assay given (in detail) in [58]. Briefly, for cell viability, HEK-293, A549 and MDA-MB- 231 cells were seeded into a 96-well plate (15,000/well) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Medium was replaced by a fresh one supplemented with NOCDs (0.1, 1, 10, 100 and 1000 µg/mL) and incubated for 24 h.

For cell proliferation, cells were seeded into a 96-well plate (1,250/well) and incubated for 24 h and then treated as described above for 24, 48, 72 and 96 h. Further, the medium was gently removed and substituted with 20 μL MTT (5 mg/mL) and 150 μL of basal medium, and incubated for 4 h. Medium was removed and DMSO + Sorensen’s glycine buffer were added (glycine 0.1 M, NaCl 0.1 M, pH 10.5) to each well. The optical density was determined on a microplate reader (Multimode plate reader VICTOR X3 Perkin Elmer, USA) at a wavelength 550 nm. Non-treated cells were used as control (of cell viability). The results are presented as percentage (relative viability of cells) with respect to the untreated control. The evaluation of the cell proliferation rate was performed using common percentage ratio, similar to the analysis presented in the literature [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis and identification of NOCDs inside cells
ce:section.ce:para: HEK-293, A549 and MDA-MB-231 cells were analyzed after staining them with Giemsa staining (Sigma Aldrich, USA) [59]. Stained cells were observed under optical microscope and analyzed using the Image-Pro Insight 9 software (Media Cybernetics Inc.). In order to visualize the presence of NOCDs inside the three different cells, HEK-293, A549 and MDA-MB- 231 were plated onto 20 mm sterile cover slips in a 6-well plate (10,000/well). The cells were incubated for 24 h, washed and then treated with different concentrations of NOCDs (0.1, 1, 10, 100 and 1000 µg/mL) during 24 h. Followed by cells’ washing with PBS and fixing with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min. The cover slip with fixed cells was examined under an epifluorescence microscopy.

The size distribution of as-prepared NOCDs was analyzed through TEM and HR-TEM (Fig. 1a). The X-ray diffraction pattern of NOCDs (Fig. 1b) exhibits a sharp peak centered around 11° (001). From the XPS study, survey scan of NOCDs clearly shows the major peaks around 284 (C1s), 400 (N1s), and 531 eV (O1s). The occurrence of N1s (≈400 eV) peak confirms the existence of nitrogen atom in as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1c). Furthermore, after deconvolution of high-resolution C1s peak of NOCDs, C–C/CC (284.2 eV), C–O (286.0 eV), CO (287.0 eV) and O–CO (288.6 eV) are observed (Fig. 2(d)), confirms the presence of carbonyl/carboxyl groups in NOCDs. Fig. 2e and f showing the deconvoluted N1s (399.5 and 401.2 eV assigned to pyridinic N and substitutional N) and O1s peaks (CO at 531.5 eV and C–O at 533.0 eV) of NOCDs, also reveal similar results. Hence, XPS results confirm the presence of N as well as oxygen-containing functional groups in NOCDs [57].

Fig. 2a reveals the strong blue fluorescence emission of NOCDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 250 to 420 nm (Fig. 2b). From PLE spectra indicating 344 nm as the optimal excitation wavelength to get high intensity emission peak at 441 nm for NOCDs (Fig. 2c, black spectra). Moreover, the NOCDs show absorption bands around 234 nm and 343 nm, which corresponding to π → π* and n → π* respectively, from the absorbance spectrum (Fig. 2c, blue spectra); the UV–vis spectra at n → π* is attributed to nitrogen as well as the strong absorption band corresponding to graphitic structure. Furthermore, the concentration dependent and saturation of PL emission was observed with the addition of 0–14 μL in NOCDs (Fig. 2d).

Fig. 3 a, reveals a small change of around ≤6% in the PL intensity after 24,000 s continuous excitation with Xenon flashlamp under 344 nm and emission at 441 nm with an average time for each measurement was 0.1 s. With respect to the already reported literature, e.g. Z. Liu et al., [47], reported nitrogen doped cellulose which show was around 5% PL signal decrement after 10,800 s irradiation under UV lamp and similarly, Mukesh L.B. et al. [48], reported fluorescent CDs via microwave carbonization of citric acid in presence of tetraethylammonium ion exhibited around 15% (of the original) in 14,400 s continuous excitation. The present study NOCDs showed higher stability than previously reported CDs.

Furthermore, photo stability was tested using UV–visible absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy studies (Fig. 3b, c) under long time exposure to 344 nm excitation and conserved under ambient conditions for over 18 months. The NOCDs show around 22% decrement in the PL signal intensity after 18 months, sample was kept in ambient conditions covered with aluminum foil. Inset of Fig. 1b reveals the visual color degradation under 365 nm UV lamp excitation respected time intervals. Similarly, the UV–vis absorbance and PLE studies also revealed an observable intensity decrement after 18 months (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3d, reveals a slight change in the visual color of NOCDs under normal day light illumination. It demonstrated that the NOCDs are highly stable after long term continuous excitation and long-term stability up to 18 months. The strong fluorescence observed after 18 months, presents great opportunities from experimental research to practical applications point of view and evolves as a promising fluorescent imaging agents for the applications in bio-imaging and biosensing.

The NOCDs show an excellent solubility and no precipitation after 18 months. Moreover, the stability of NOCDs was monitored by evaluating the fluorescence and UV–visible absorbance intensity, for over a period of 18 months. A noteworthy alteration in the intensity was observed when excited at 360 nm, which is similar to absorbance studies (Fig. 3c). The aqueous solution of NOCDs revealed a homogeneous phase over a long time deprived of any visible floating particles or precipitation at ambient conditions. The overall outcomes elucidate that the as-prepared NOCDs revealed good dispersibility and promising optical properties in aqueous solution (Fig. 3d).

We measured the fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared NOCDs as a function of temperature from 25 to 70 °C (Fig. 4 ). With increasing temperature, the intensity of fluorescence exhibits a monotonous decrease. Basically, possible processes resulting in photoexcited electron relaxation in the NOCDs include radiative relaxation, auger nonradiative scattering, and thermally activated trapping on the surface and/or defect/impurity states. In our experiments, the excitation intensity was very low, and thus, auger scattering could be ruled out. Therefore, the nonradiative relaxation is most likely the contributing factor due to the thermal activation of nonradiative trapping, which is often observed in semiconductor bulk, quantum dots, and core-shell structured quantum dots [60]. At each temperature, the PL spectrum exhibits symmetric peaks, suggesting single fluorophores or luminescent species.

The reversibility and stability of the temperature-dependent fluorescence of NOCDs were evaluated through thermal cycling experiments (Fig. 4c). The thermal reversibility of NOCDs, was assessed by measuring recovery through multiple heating and cooling cycles (from 25 to 70 °C). The results show that the initial fluorescence intensity does not significantly change due to multiple heating and cooling cycles indicating their ability to recover the thermal state.

Apart from the temperature dependent fluorescence behaviour, emission signal is also very sensitive to pH (Fig. 5 ). The PL intensity of NOCDs is gradually increasing with increasing pH (pH = 2 to 3), but changes pronouncedly and inversely in the deep acidic and basic regions. When pH is tuned from 9 to 3, the PL signal intensity decreases with pH but there is no observable shift in the emission peak. At pH 2, a drastic change with two distinct peaks with a red shift (around 25 nm) in the emission wavelength, is observed. The quenching of the NOCDs’ PL signal intensity could be due to the aggregation (possibly-OH combines with –COOH present on NOCDs surface) of NOCDs. There is an excellent linear dependence from pH 3 to 9 with R2 = 0.989 (Fig. 5b). After the pH value is restored to 3 (Fig. 5c), PL spectra are also restored, which indicates that the pH-dependent PL behaviour is reversible, similar to the observation with blue fluorescent GQDs and CNPs. This pH effect indicates that the fluorescent species in the NOCDs should have basic sites relevant to the blue emission because the fluorescence is quenched in the acidic media but restored in the basic media. Fig. 5d shows zeta potential values of NOCDs at different pH values, i.e., pH 2 to 9 shows drastic increment in the surface charge of NOCDs from −8 ± 2 mV to −54 ± 2 mV respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of organic pollutants using NOCDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Among various organic dyes, azo dyes gain more attention due to their at least one chromophore, exhibit resonance of electrons, and conjugated system (i.e. structure with alternating double and single bonds) and which is a stabilizing force and versatility in organic compounds. Hence, the direct determination of these dyes in wastewater effluents is difficult, expensive and moreover, it necessitates special procedures and techniques [41,61].

The presence of MB (cationic dye) from 25 µM to 0.3 mM in NOCDs solution (Fig. 7a), resulted in PL signal intensity quenching of up to 70% (with respect to the signal from pristine NOCDs) with blue shift in the peak position around 9 nm (441–432 nm). The correlation coefficient R2 was calculated to be 0.976 using first order pseudo reaction (Fig. 8). Full width and half maxima (FWHM) is presented in Fig. 9b. From Fig. 7a and 8, left side peak (410–409 nm) reveals negligible shift as compared with right side (488–472 nm) peak for all the concentrations of MB. The difference in the FWHM of PL spectra corresponding to minimum and maximum MB detection is ≈12 nm.

The presence of Rh6G (cationic dye) from 5 µM to 0.2 mM in NOCDs solution (Fig. 7b), resulted in PL signal intensity quenching up to 70% (with respect to the PL signal from pristine NOCDs) with a blue shift of around 12 nm (442 to 428 nm) in the peak position. The correlation coefficient R2 was calculated to be 0.978 using first order pseudo reaction (Fig. 8) (measured FWHM is presented in Fig. 9c). From Figs. 7b and 8, left side peak is found to reveal an insignificant shift as compared with right side peak (shifted from 485 to 448 nm) for all the concentrations of Rh6G. The difference in the FWHM of PL spectra corresponding to minimum and maximum Rh6G detection is ≈34 nm.

The presence of broad range of MO (anionic dye) concentration (from 250 nM to 1.25 mM in N-CDots solution; Fig. 7c), there was a PL signal intensity quenching up to 90% to the NOCDs original signal with blue shift in the peak position around 14 nm (441–427 nm). The correlation coefficient R2 was calculated to be 0.986 using first order linear relationship (Fig. 8). Furthermore, the full width and half maxima (FWHM) was measured and presented in Fig. 9a. From Figs. 7c and 8, left side peak (410–405 nm) is less blue shift as compared with right side (487–475 nm) peak for all the concentrations of MO. The difference in the FWHM of minimum to maximum MO detection is ≈8 nm.

The presence of BrPB (anionic dye) from 2.5 µM to 0.4 mM in NOCDs solution (Fig. 7d), there was a PL signal intensity quenching up to 90% to the NOCDs original signal with blue shift in the peak position around 12 nm (441–427 nm). The correlation coefficient R2 was calculated to be 0.992 using second order pseudo reaction (Fig. 8d). Furthermore, the full width and half maxima (FWHM) was calculated and presented in Fig. 9d. From Figs. 7d and 8, left side peak is negligible red shift (410–412 nm) as compared with right side (484–460 nm) peak for all the concentrations of BrPB. The difference in the FWHM of minimum to maximum BrPB detection is ≈28 nm.

The minimum detection limit was calculated using LOD = 3 × SD/m (where Signal to noise ratio is 3, Standard deviation SD and their corresponding linear range slope m), with sensitive detection of toxic organic pollutants such as methyl orange, bromophenol blue, methylene blue, and rhodamine 6G with a detection limit of 38.3 nM, 2.0 µM, 1.5 µM, and 1.0 µM respectively (from Fig. 7) [62,63].

The minimum quantification limit was calculated using LOQ = 3.33 × LOD, with sensitive quantification of toxic organic pollutants such as methyl orange, bromophenol blue, methylene blue, and rhodamine 6G with a detection limit of 128 nM, 6.7 µM, 5.0 µM, and 3.3 µM respectively (from Fig. 7) [64].

The sensing experiments were carried out in batches as a function of NOCDs concentration and pH of aqueous organic pollutants with a fixed total volume (rest of the parameters were kept as mentioned in the experimental Section 2.8). The reaction mixtures were stirred at ambient conditions, after maintaining the respective pH and NOCDs concentrations before analyzing in a Fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on organic dyes sensing
Effect of NOCDs’ concentration on sensing of organic dyes
Possible mechanism for the sensing of organic pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: As per the above results, the minimum detection was showed in anionic organic dyes (as compared to the cationic dyes). The NOCDs absorbance spectra (black spectra) intensity increases with the addition of MB, MO, BrPB and Rh6G, which significantly overlaps with the PL and UV–Vis spectra of NOCDs (Figs. 2c and 10). This indicates that the fluorescence of NOCDs is quenched by MB, MO, BrPB and Rh6G due to the inner filter effect (IFE). Till now there is only one report [65] on detection of methylene blue using relatively toxic 1,2,4-benzenetriamine dihydrochloride based red-emitting carbon dots, in which similar detection mechanism (IFE) was reported (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility studies of NOCDs in tumor and non-tumor cells
ce:section.ce:para: As cell viability is a main step in usual cell manipulation and a requirement for subsequent processing/analysis, Fig. 15 shows the effect of the proposed carbon dots on the cell viability in different kinds of cell lines. HEK293 cells (non-tumoral cell line) derived from human embryonic kidney cells showed slight reduction in cell viability with an increase in the concentration from 10 to 500 µg/mL (15%) as compared with control group (p < 0.05). However, when cells were treated with 1000 µg/mL of NOCDs concentration, a significant reduction (approx. 23%) in cell viability is observed [58,59]. A549 cells (tumoral cell line) derived from a human pulmonary adenocarcinoma also showed a slight variation in cell viability with all concentrations, however they were not statistically significant. MDA-MB-231 cells (another tumoral cell line) derived from a human breast cancer showed a significant reduction on cell viability at 1000 µg/mL concentration. In general, the results demonstrated that non-tumoral cells appear to be relatively more sensitive to the possible toxic effects of the synthesized NOCDs (Fig. 15).

The cell proliferation (CP) analysis showed that CP rate was affected after 48 h in all cell lines at concentrations of 100, 500 and 1000 µg/mL, however A549 cells were found to be relatively more affected. After 72 h, there was an important reduction in cell proliferation rate in all cell lines, i.e., with at a maximum tested concentration of NOCDs (1 mg/ml), HEK 293, A549 and MDA-MB-231 cells were found to reduce by 45%, 62% and 37% respectively. After 96 h, the HEK 293 cells were found to be affected relatively more than the other cells, with an observed reduction of 78% in the cell proliferation rate, and both tumoral cell lines were found with a reduced cell proliferation rate of ≈54%. These results indicate that non-tumoral cells could be affected in its proliferation rate by the presence of carbon quantum dots at concentrations higher than 10 µg/mL (Table 3 ).

Morphological analysis revealed a reduction in the number of HEK 293 cells and presence of cell death at 1000 µg/mL, non-morphological changes were observed in live cells with less concentration. Although A549 cells did not show morphological changes at any concentration as compared with control group, MDA-MB-231 cells showed the presence of vacuoles of different sizes at concentration of ≥100 µg/mL and the cells revealed an elongated shape and reduction in number at 1000 µg/mL (Fig. 16 (upper part)). The presence of carbon quantum dots was evident by the presence of green color inside the cells when observed under an epifluorescence microscopy. All cell lines showed the presence of quantum dots and the intensity of fluorescence increased with the concentration of carbon quantum dots. Additionally, it was interesting to observe (Fig. 16 (upper part)) the absence of quantum dots inside the cell nuclei (the presence was only evident in cytoplasm of all cell lines) [59].

Present results demonstrated that carbon quantum dots produced different effects on human cell lines, causing more cytotoxic effects mainly in HEK293 cells. These quantum dots may affect cell proliferation rate in vitro in a dose-depended manner in tumoral and non-tumoral cells. At concentration lower than 10 µg/mL, the carbon quantum dots can have potential applications in bio-imaging.

Fig. 16 (lower part of the figure) shows the effect of carbon quantum dots on cell morphology and identification of NOCDs into cells. Morphological analysis of each cell line and treated with carbon quantum dots for 24 h reveals a clear fluorescent microscopic visualization of NOCDs within human cell lines at concentrations higher than 10 µg/mL for HEK-293, A549, whereas for MDA-MB-231 cells 1 µg/ml was found to be sufficient. Fluorescence images (green) showing presence of quantum dots in the cytoplasm of all cell lines confirms the applicability of the proposed CDs as stable biomarkers at considerably low concentrations.

With respect to the novel fluorescence sensing probes reported recently [47,48] the nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots-based sensing probes reported in the present work have several advantages, such as (i) outstanding photostability over continuous excitation with Xenon flashlamp and under ambient conditions for up to 18 months, (ii) pH and temperature sensor response revealing a linear, reversible trend (iii) highly sensitive and selective detection of toxic organic pollutants. Additionally, in vitro imaging on three different kinds of cells confirms their biocompatibility.

In conclusion, N doped carbon dots with excitation wavelength independent PL properties were synthesized using a simple one-step hydrothermal route. The luminescent NOCDs exhibited an excellent sensing properties with sensitive detection of toxic colorants like methyl orange, bromophenol blue, methylene blue, and rhodamine 6G with a detection limit of 38.3 nM, 2.0 µM, 1.5 µM, and 1.0 µM respectively. The strong dependence of the optical properties on temperature and pH can have potential applications in chemical and biosensing applications as detecting emitters. From cell proliferation and imaging in tumoral and non-tumoral cells (HEK-293, A549 and MDA-MB-231), observation of strong fluorescence after 24 h of incubation at different concentrations of NOCDs (0.1, 1, 10, 100, 500, 1000 µg/mL) verified the biocompatibility and capacity of the CDs to provide enough contrast in tissue samples or in-vivo studies. The scalability and sensitivity of the proposed NOCDs open great opportunities from experimental research to practical applications to develop them as promising candidates as fluorescent optical sensing and imaging nanoprobes for the applications in chemical/bio-sensing and bio-imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: VA acknowledges the financial grant from CONACyT Basic Sciences project A1-S-30393.
